task: A storm knocked out power and I’m trying to think through the next day or two without making a mess of things. Can you help me put together a practical order of operations for keeping the important stuff safe, getting by without electricity, and handling any follow-up paperwork if something gets damaged?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s a practical order of operations you can use.

## 1) First: make sure people and the house are safe
- Check for injuries.
- If you smell gas, hear hissing, see sparks, or see downed lines: stay away and call emergency services / utility.
- If water is coming in, move people and valuables out of that area first.
- Don’t use candles if you can avoid it; use flashlights.

## 2) Protect the most important things
Focus on the stuff that is hardest to replace or most likely to be damaged next:
- Documents: IDs, insurance papers, passports, deeds, medical records
- Electronics: laptops, routers, external drives, chargers
- Medications and medical devices
- Cash, checkbooks, keys
- Photos / irreplaceable items
- Anything already wet or at risk of getting wetter

If damage is starting:
- Move items up and away from floors, windows, and leaks
- Take quick photos/video before moving too much
- Use towels, bins, tarps, or plastic sheeting to limit further damage

## 3) Get through the outage safely
### Food
- Keep fridge/freezer doors closed as much as possible.
- Use food in this order:
  1. Perishables that will spoil first
  2. Fridge items
  3. Freezer items if they start thawing
- If you’re unsure, throw it out.

### Power and devices
- Unplug sensitive electronics if power is unstable.
- Use battery banks, car chargers, or a generator only if safe and outdoors per instructions.
- Conserve phone battery:
  - Low power mode
  - Limit screen use
  - Keep one phone charged for emergency updates

### Water and heat/cooling
- Fill tubs, pitchers, and containers if water still works.
- Dress for the temperature:
  - Layer up if cold
  - Stay in the coolest part of the house if hot
- Avoid running anything that creates fumes indoors.

### Daily basics
- Make a short list of what you need for 24–48 hours:
  - Water
  - Food
  - Meds
  - Flashlights/batteries
  - Phone charging
  - Pet supplies
  - Ice/cooler if needed

## 4) Watch for damage that gets worse over time
Check the house once conditions are safe:
- Roof, ceiling stains, attic leaks
- Windows and doors
- Basement or crawlspace flooding
- Siding, gutters, fence, trees on the house
- Appliances and electrical panels
- Mold risk if anything stayed wet

If something is damaged:
- Prevent more damage if you can do it safely
- Save broken parts if they might matter later
- Keep receipts for tarps, fans, hotel stays, food loss, batteries, repairs

## 5) Document everything for insurance or later claims
Do this before major cleanup if possible:
- Take wide photos of the whole area
- Take close-ups of each item or damage spot
- Record date/time if you can
- Make a simple list:
  - What happened
  - What was damaged
  - What you did to prevent more damage
  - Any costs you had because of the storm

### Good claim notes to keep
- Power outage start/end time
- Any flood/wind/tree damage
- Utility company or emergency alerts
- Contractor estimates
- Hotel or food receipts if relevant
- Any communication with the insurer

## 6) Handle insurance and paperwork
If damage looks real:
- Contact your insurer sooner rather than later
- Ask what they need and whether they want photos before cleanup
- Ask about:
  - Temporary repairs
  - Loss of use / hotel coverage
  - Food spoilage
  - Deductible
  - Claim number and next steps

Keep everything in one place:
- Photos
- Receipts
- Notes
- Claim number
- Names and dates of who you spoke with

## 7) Simple priority order for the next day or two
If you want a quick rule:
1. People safe
2. Prevent more damage
3. Keep food/water/meds going
4. Document damage
5. Notify insurer / utility / landlord if needed
6. Start cleanup only after you’ve recorded what matters

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page storm outage checklist** you can keep on your phone.

user_simulator.text: Pretty good, but too generic. I want it tighter and more usable, like real steps for the first 2 hours, then overnight, then next morning. Also include what to do if the basement starts taking water, because that’s the part that turns ugly fast. And the paperwork part — spell out exactly what to photograph and what receipts matter. Keep it plain.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. Here’s a tighter, plain-language version you can actually use.

# Storm outage order of operations

## First 2 hours
### 1) Make it safe
- Check for injuries.
- If you smell gas, hear hissing, see sparks, or see downed wires: stay away and call emergency help / utility.
- If water is near outlets, the panel, or appliances: don’t touch it.
- Use flashlights, not candles.

### 2) Save the stuff that’s hardest to replace
Grab these first:
- IDs, passports, insurance papers, deed/lease, medical records
- Meds, glasses, chargers, hearing aids, medical devices
- Laptop, phone, hard drive, backup drive
- Cash, checkbook, keys
- Photos and irreplaceable items

Put them in one dry bin or bag and move them up high.

### 3) If the basement starts taking water
Do this in order:
1. **Stay out if water is near the panel, outlets, or plugged-in equipment.**
2. **Move people and valuables out first.**
3. **Shut off power to the basement only if the panel is dry and you can reach it safely.**
4. **Move box items, rugs, paper, and electronics up higher fast.**
5. **Put towels, bins, or plastic at the entry to slow more water.**
6. **Take photos before moving too much.**
7. **If water is rising fast, call for help and leave if needed.**

Do **not** go into standing water if you don’t know whether electricity is live.

### 4) Keep the basics going
- Keep fridge and freezer closed.
- Put phone on low power mode.
- Start charging banks and devices if you have power from a car or battery.
- Fill containers with water if tap water still works.
- Set aside meds, water, food, pet supplies for 24–48 hours.

---

## Overnight
### 1) Protect the house from getting worse
- Put buckets under leaks.
- Move furniture away from wet walls or windows.
- Lift anything off the floor in the basement.
- If safe, open curtains and doors a bit for airflow.
- If it’s cold, keep heat loss low by closing off rooms you don’t need.

### 2) Watch food and water
- Don’t open the fridge unless you need something.
- Use perishable food first if it’s warming up.
- If food sat above safe temp too long, toss it.
- Keep bottled water handy.

### 3) Sleep setup
- Charge one phone fully.
- Keep flashlight, shoes, keys, meds, and glasses by the bed.
- If the basement is wet, don’t sleep down there.
- If there’s any chance of flooding getting worse, sleep where you can leave fast.

### 4) Keep notes
Write down:
- When power went out
- When water started, if it did
- What got wet or damaged
- Anything you had to throw out
- Who you called

---

## Next morning
### 1) Quick damage check
Look for:
- Water in basement or crawlspace
- Wet drywall, ceilings, floors, insulation
- Roof leaks or ceiling stains
- Broken windows, siding, gutters
- Fallen trees or branches
- Appliances that got wet
- Electrical panel issues

If anything looks unsafe, don’t force it.

### 2) Start cleanup only after documenting
Before you throw or move much:
- Take wide photos of each room
- Take close-ups of damage
- Save broken parts if possible
- Keep wet items until you know they’re not needed for the claim

### 3) Call the right people
- Utility company if power is still out too long or lines are down
- Landlord if you rent
- Insurance company if there’s real damage
- Contractor only for emergency repairs, not full cleanup yet

---

# What to photograph
Take these photos in this order:

## Wide shots
- Each damaged room from all corners
- Basement as a whole
- Outside of the house if there’s visible storm damage
- Any tree, branch, or debris impact

## Close-ups
- Water line on walls
- Wet carpet, flooring, boxes, furniture
- Damaged appliances
- Electrical panel area
- Broken windows, siding, roof damage
- Anything moldy, warped, or stained
- Items you had to throw out

## Proof shots
- Power outage condition if relevant
- Flood level marks
- Utility alerts or storm warnings on your phone if useful
- Serial numbers on major damaged items if visible

## For each item, try to capture
- What it is
- Where it was
- How it was damaged
- Whether it was already wet, broken, or ruined

---

# What receipts matter
Keep receipts for anything caused by the storm or needed because of the storm:

- Tarps, plastic sheeting, duct tape
- Fans, dehumidifiers, wet/dry vac rental
- Batteries, flashlights, extension cords
- Bottled water, ice, cooler, ice packs
- Hotel or temporary lodging
- Restaurant meals if you couldn’t cook
- Laundry costs if needed because of the outage
- Emergency repairs
- Towing or vehicle-related storm damage
- Contractor estimates
- Replacement of spoiled food
- Locksmith if keys were lost in the storm

Save:
- Date
- Vendor name
- Item or service
- Amount

---

# Simple rule for the next 48 hours
1. **Keep people safe**
2. **Stop more damage**
3. **Save the important stuff**
4. **Document before cleanup**
5. **Keep receipts**
6. **Call insurance / landlord / utility**

If you want, I can turn this into a **super-short checklist version** you can keep in your phone notes.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still a little too neat, but usable.

What I’d want next is the ugly version — what to do if the basement is already taking water and the power may still be live. Also, for the paperwork, I want a real claim list, not just “take photos.” Like: room by room, item by item, what matters and what doesn’t. And tell me the order — what gets done before cleanup, what can wait until morning, what you’d actually keep in one envelope.

Plain is good. No fluff.

turns.agent_response:
## Ugly version: basement taking water, power may still be live

### Right now
- **Do not go into standing water** if there is any chance outlets, cords, appliances, or the panel are live.
- **Do not touch the panel** if you have to stand in water to reach it.
- **Get people out first.**
- **Get pets out.**
- **Grab only the high-value dry stuff if you can do it without stepping into water.**
- **Turn off power to the basement only if the main panel is dry, reachable, and you can do it without standing in water.**
- **If you are not sure, leave it alone and call the utility / electrician.**

### If water is creeping in
Do this in order:
1. **Move people out of the basement.**
2. **Pull plugs and move cords only if the floor is dry enough to do it safely.**
3. **Move these first:**
   - documents
   - meds
   - laptops / hard drives
   - photos
   - cash / checkbook / keys
4. **Get boxes, paper, rugs, and anything on the floor up onto shelves, stairs, or counters.**
5. **Put towels, bins, or plastic at the doorway if it helps, but don’t waste time fighting water you can’t stop.**
6. **Take quick photos before you move too much.**
7. **If the water is rising, stop and get out.**

### If water is already in the basement
- Treat the basement like a live hazard until proven otherwise.
- **No bare feet. No wading.**
- If you must go in and it seems safe, keep it short and stay away from cords, appliances, and the panel.
- **Do not run a shop vac, sump pump, or extension cord through water unless you know it is safe.**
- If the furnace, water heater, washer, dryer, or electrical panel got wet, assume it needs inspection before use.

### What to save first from the basement
- insurance papers
- passports / IDs
- deed / lease
- meds
- laptop / external drive
- photos
- cash / checkbook
- spare keys
- family records
- anything already packed and easy to grab

### What does not matter yet
- Holiday stuff
- Old clothes
- random boxes
- broken furniture
- anything cheap and replaceable

---

## Claim paperwork: what to do, in order

## Before cleanup
### 1) Make one claim pile
Put everything in one envelope, folder, or zip bag:
- insurance card / policy page
- claim number
- notes
- receipts
- printed photos if you have them
- business cards / names of people you spoke to
- contractor estimates later

### 2) Write the basic story
On one page, write:
- date and time power went out
- date and time water started, if it did
- where the water came from
- what rooms were affected
- what got wet or damaged
- what you moved or threw out
- who you called

### 3) Photograph the house before cleanup
Take photos in this order:
- outside damage
- basement overall
- each room affected
- each wall with water line or stains
- each item damaged
- any appliances or panel that got wet
- anything you had to throw out

---

## Room by room: what matters
### Basement
Photograph:
- full room from each corner
- water line on wall
- floor covering
- stored boxes
- furniture
- appliances
- furnace / water heater / washer / dryer
- electrical panel area
- sump pump area if you have one

Keep notes on:
- how high the water got
- what sat in water
- what had to be moved
- what broke or stopped working

### First floor / living areas
Photograph:
- wet floors
- baseboards
- drywall stains
- furniture legs
- rugs
- electronics
- anything blown in through windows or doors

### Kitchen
Photograph:
- fridge and freezer before opening too much
- spoiled food if you throw it out
- appliances if power surge affected them
- cabinets if water got in

### Bedrooms / office
Photograph:
- mattresses if wet
- dressers
- clothing
- computers
- paper files
- chargers and power strips

### Outside
Photograph:
- roof damage
- broken branches on house
- siding
- gutters
- fences
- fallen trees
- blocked driveway / access
- damaged AC unit or meter area

---

## Item by item: what to list
For each important damaged item, write:
- what it is
- brand / model if known
- where it was
- what happened to it
- whether it still works
- rough age if you know it
- estimated value if you can guess

### Focus on these first
- electronics
- appliances
- furniture with real value
- rugs / flooring
- stored tools
- medical devices
- documents
- photos
- food loss

### Don’t waste time listing every cheap thing
- socks
- cheap dishes
- old bins
- junk drawer stuff
- low-value clothing

---

## What receipts matter
Keep receipts for anything you bought because of the storm:
- batteries
- flashlights
- tarps
- plastic sheeting
- duct tape
- fans
- dehumidifier rental
- wet/dry vac rental
- bottled water
- ice
- coolers
- hotel / motel
- restaurant meals if you couldn’t cook
- laundry
- emergency repairs
- plumber / electrician / water mitigation
- towing
- locksmith
- spoiled food replacement

On the receipt, make sure you can match:
- date
- store or company
- what it was for
- amount

---

## What can wait until morning
- full cleanup
- sorting every box
- deep inventory
- calling every contractor
- arguing with the insurer
- replacing normal household stuff

## What should happen before cleanup
- get people safe
- stop more damage if safe
- photograph everything
- save important papers and electronics
- keep receipts
- make the basic damage list
- call insurer / landlord / utility if needed

---

## What I’d keep in one envelope
- insurance policy page
- claim number
- one-page storm notes
- printed photos or photo list
- receipts
- contractor estimates
- contact names / phone numbers
- copies of IDs if needed
- any letters from insurer or utility

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page checklist with boxes** and a **blank claim log** you can fill in by hand.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the version I wanted. Basement part is the right kind of ugly, and the claim list is clear enough to use without squinting at it. One envelope, before cleanup, what can wait — that’s the part people usually mess up.

I’d probably keep the one-page notes, claim number, receipts, and the photos list together. Maybe a copy of the policy page too. The rest can sit until morning.

Good enough.
